commit.message:
feat: snapshot-based fast startup (#409)

Wire existing snapshot infrastructure for startup optimization:

- --emit-snapshot=<path>: serialize engine globals to disk
- --snapshot=<path>: deserialize globals on startup (skip bootstrap)
- Graceful fallback to install_globals() if snapshot is invalid

The snapshot serialization (StartupSnapshot, serialize_globals,
deserialize_globals) already existed. This wires it into the st8
CLI with command-line flags for build-time snapshot generation
and fast startup loading.

Target: startup from ~50ms to <5ms (V8: ~3ms).

commit.message:
feat(veu-ghost): Phase 4 — Ghost Network (minimal P2P sync)

Implement the veu-ghost Swift Package, providing peer-to-peer artifact
synchronization over local Wi-Fi with no central server.

Discovery:
- LocalPulse: mDNS/Bonjour service (_veu-ghost._tcp) using NWBrowser
  and NWListener.  TXT record contains HMAC-SHA-256 Circle topic hash
  so only Circle members can recognize each other.

Transport:
- GhostConnection: NWConnection wrapper with length-prefixed framing
  and AES-256-GCM envelope encryption using the Circle Key.
- GhostMessage: Codable protocol messages (SyncRequest, SyncResponse,
  ArtifactPush, BurnNotice, Ack) per SYNC.md spec.

Sync:
- VectorClock: per-peer sequence tracking with merge, delta calculation,
  dominance and concurrency detection.
- SyncEngine: delta-sync orchestrator — exchange vector clocks, calculate
  missing artifacts, push deltas, update Ledger (markSynced/purgeArtifact).

Coordinator:
- GhostNode: top-level class owning LocalPulse + SyncEngine.  Auto-connects
  to discovered peers, handles inbound connections, drives sync lifecycle.

Tests: 44 tests passing (vector clock operations, message serialization,
AES-256-GCM envelope seal/open, sync engine clock management, burn
propagation, GhostNode lifecycle).

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

feat: wire GC to real allocations with thread-local heap (#403)

Add gc::runtime module providing the thread-local allocation and
collection API that wires the existing GC infrastructure to the runtime:

- gc_alloc<T>(): typed allocation returning GcPtr<T>
- gc_alloc_raw(): low-level Layout-based allocation
- gc_collect(): trigger minor (young-gen) collection
- gc_safepoint(): automatic collection when threshold exceeded
- gc_stats()/gc_set_threshold(): runtime statistics and tuning
- with_heap(): direct heap access for advanced operations

The Heap, SemiSpace, OldSpace, LargeObjectSpace, GcPtr, GcObject,
HandleScope, Local, and Persistent types already existed. This commit
provides the thread-local runtime glue so that the interpreter can
allocate GC-managed objects and trigger collection cycles.

commit.message:
fix: self-update process lock on Windows (#319)

* fix: self-update process lock on Windows via detached updater

On Windows, the running jdai.exe is file-locked by the OS so
dotnet tool update -g JD.AI fails in-process with 'access denied'.

Solution: platform-aware update strategy
- Windows: DetachedUpdater.Launch() writes a temp .bat script and opens
  it in a new cmd.exe window via UseShellExecute. The script waits 3s
  then runs dotnet tool update -g JD.AI and shows the result.
- Unix: unchanged — OS allows replacing a running binary's backing file.

Changes:
- Add LaunchedDetached bool to InstallResult record
- New DetachedUpdater static class (src/JD.AI.Core/Installation/)
- DotnetToolStrategy.ApplyAsync: Windows → DetachedUpdater, Unix → existing
- UpdateChecker.ApplyUpdateAsync: surface LaunchedDetached in tuple
- UpdateCliHandler: handle LaunchedDetached (print message, return 0)
- UpdatePrompter: handle LaunchedDetached with appropriate UX messaging
- SlashCommandRouter /update: updated response text
- 6 unit tests covering InstallResult fields and Windows launch path

Fixes: jdai update / /update failing on Windows with file-lock error

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

* fix: harden DetachedUpdater — injection protection, PID polling, atomic cache

Address code review findings on PR #319:

PackageId/Version injection protection:
- Validate packageId with SafePackageIdPattern (^[A-Za-z0-9._-]+$) before
  embedding in any generated script — rejects shell metacharacters like & ; | >
- Validate targetVersion with SafeVersionPattern before embedding
- Return InstallResult(Success: false) with descriptive message on validation failure

PID-based waiting instead of hardcoded timeout:
- Embed parent process PID statically in bat/sh script at generation time
- Windows bat: :wait_loop with 	asklist /FI 'PID eq %PARENT_PID%' polling
- Unix sh: while kill -0 \ 2>/dev/null; do sleep 1; done
- Eliminates race condition where 2s timeout could expire before jdai exits

Bat script improvements:
- Self-delete via del /f /q %~f0 (was del /f /q scriptPath — now uses bat
  variable expansion to avoid temp file accumulation)
- UseShellExecute = false for outer cmd.exe (start creates visible window)
- cmd.exe /c start opens visible window without extra shell wrapper layer

Atomic WriteCache in UpdateChecker:
- Write to .tmp side-file then File.Move(overwrite: true) — atomic on
  NTFS and ext4, prevents truncated JSON if two jdai instances start together

Daemon UpdateService.RunToolUpdateAsync:
- On Windows: call DetachedUpdater.Launch() instead of in-process
  dotnet tool update; daemon then calls StopApplication() per normal flow;
  SCM/systemd service recovery restarts the daemon with the new binary
- Unix path unchanged

Tests: 16 tests covering record fields, injection validation, safe ID/version
patterns, and Windows launch behavior (skipped on non-Windows CI)

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
feat: add Display P3 and BT.2020 wide-gamut bitmap contexts

Add permutations 16 (Display P3) and 17 (BT.2020) to exercise
wide-gamut color space paths in CoreGraphics:

- Display P3: CGColorSpaceCreateWithName(kCGColorSpaceDisplayP3)
  25% wider gamut than sRGB, used on modern Apple displays
- BT.2020: CGColorSpaceCreateWithName(kCGColorSpaceITUR_2020)
  Ultra-wide gamut for UHD content, stress-tests color management

Both contexts use RGBA 8-bit with premultiplied alpha, output to
PNG/JPEG/TIFF/GIF with ICC variants. Total: 15 → 17 bitmap contexts.

Fix hlx_test_results error when no .trx files found (#5)

* Fix hlx_test_results error handling and add xUnit XML support

P0: Catch HelixException in MCP tool handler and rethrow as McpException
so the MCP SDK surfaces the actual error message instead of a generic
'An error occurred invoking hlx_test_results'.

P1: Add xUnit XML format parsing alongside TRX. When no .trx files are
found, auto-discovery falls back to *.xml and detects xUnit format
(<assemblies>/<assembly>/<collection>/<test> schema). This supports
ASP.NET Core projects that use --logger xunit producing TestResults.xml.

The fallback path uses best-effort parsing (skips malformed/unrecognized
XML files) while the TRX path remains strict, preserving XXE detection
via DtdProcessing.Prohibit.

Closes #4

* fix: use Lazy<T> in CacheStoreFactory to prevent concurrent SQLite init race

ConcurrentDictionary.GetOrAdd can invoke the factory multiple times for
the same key under contention. This caused SqliteCacheStore.InitializeSchema()
to race on Windows, producing ArgumentOutOfRangeException from SQLitePCL.

Wrap the factory in Lazy<T> to guarantee single-invocation semantics.

commit.message:
Clean up plugin injections on uninstall and add project-level injection management UI (#484)

Uninstalling a plugin left orphaned skills, instruction blocks,
permission rules, and MCP server entries in `.clubhouse/settings.json`
and `.clubhouse/skills/` indefinitely. There was also no UI surface to
audit or remove what a plugin had injected at the project level.

## Core cleanup logic (`plugin-discovery.ts`)

Three new functions added to the main process:

- **`cleanupProjectPluginInjections(pluginId, projectPath)`** — removes
all project-level artifacts for a plugin:
  - Deletes `plugin-{id}-*` source skills and agent templates
- Strips `<!-- plugin:{id}:start -->…end` blocks from project agent
default instructions
  - Removes `/* plugin:{id} */` tagged permission rules (allow + deny)
  - Removes `plugin-{id}-*` MCP server entries from `mcpJson`
- Deletes the `_agentconfig:{id}` storage directory under
`.clubhouse/plugin-data/`

- **`listProjectPluginInjections(pluginId, projectPath)`** — returns a
structured summary of what a plugin has injected (skill names, template
names, instruction presence, permission counts, MCP server names)

- **`listOrphanedPluginIds(projectPath, knownPluginIds)`** — scans
`_agentconfig:*` storage dirs, instruction comment markers, and
permission rule comments to surface injection remnants from plugins no
longer installed

## Uninstall flow (`PluginListSettings.tsx`)

`handleUninstall` / `handleUninstallAll` now call
`cleanupProjectInjections` across **all known projects** before removing
the plugin from disk.

## Management UI (`PluginListSettings.tsx`)

- **`PluginInjectionsPanel`** — rendered inside each plugin row in
project context; lists active injections (skills, templates,
instructions, permissions, MCP servers) with a "Remove all" button and
inline error feedback
- **`OrphanInjectionsBanner`** — shown at the top of the project plugin
list when injections from uninstalled plugins are detected; provides
per-plugin "Clean up" and "Clean all" actions

## IPC surface

| Channel | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `plugin:list-project-injections` | renderer → main | Query what a
plugin has injected |
| `plugin:cleanup-project-injections` | renderer → main | Remove all
injections for a plugin in a project |
| `plugin:list-orphaned-plugin-ids` | renderer → main | Find injection
markers from uninstalled plugins |

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>


----

*This section details on the original issue you should resolve*

<issue_title>[MEDIUM] Plugin prompt and skill injections are not cleaned
up on uninstall and have no management UI</issue_title>
<issue_description>## Problem

When a plugin that injects agent configuration (skills, instructions,
permission rules, MCP servers) is uninstalled, **none of the
project-level injections are cleaned up**. Additionally, there is **no
UI anywhere in Settings** to view, audit, or remove what a plugin has
injected at the project level.

This means orphaned prompt injections silently persist in
`.clubhouse/settings.json` and `.clubhouse/skills/`, continuing to
influence agent behavior indefinitely after the plugin is gone.

### Real-world example

The **buddy-system** plugin was uninstalled, but all of these artifacts
remain:

**1. Injected instructions in `.clubhouse/settings.json` (line 5)**
```
<!-- plugin:buddy-system:start -->
You are participating in a Buddy System group "noble-vanguard". Check your assignment at
/tmp/.clubhouse/plugin-data/buddy-system/workspace/groups/mm85zhy4_y3v3m9/assignments/...
<!-- plugin:buddy-system:end -->
```

**2. Injected permission rules in `.clubhouse/settings.json` (lines
36-37)**
```json
"Bash(read:/tmp/.clubhouse/plugin-data/buddy-system/workspace/groups/**) /* plugin:buddy-system */",
"Bash(write:/tmp/.clubhouse/plugin-data/buddy-system/workspace/groups/mm85zhy4_y3v3m9/**) /* plugin:buddy-system */"
```

**3. Orphaned skill file** —
`.clubhouse/skills/plugin-buddy-system-buddy-system-mission/SKILL.md`
(55 lines of buddy-system instructions still injected into every agent)

**4. Orphaned metadata** —
`.clubhouse/plugin-data/_agentconfig:buddy-system/kv/injected-skills.json`

## Root Cause

### Uninstall handler only cleans global state

`src/main/services/plugin-discovery.ts:58-82`:
```typescript
export async function uninstallPlugin(pluginId: string): Promise<void> {
  const pluginDir = path.join(getCommunityPluginsDir(), pluginId);
  // ... removes ~/.clubhouse/plugins/{pluginId} ...

  // Clean up the plugin's data directory (storage + files dataDir)
  const dataDir = path.join(getGlobalPluginDataDir(), pluginId);
  try {
    await fs.promises.rm(dataDir, { recursive: true, force: true });
  } catch {
    // Best-effort — data dir may not exist
  }
}
```

It removes:
- `~/.clubhouse/plugins/{pluginId}` (the plugin itself)
- `~/.clubhouse/plugin-data/{pluginId}` (global data dir)

It does **NOT** touch:
- `.clubhouse/settings.json` (instructions, permissions, MCP config)
- `.clubhouse/skills/plugin-{pluginId}-*/` (injected skill files)
- `.clubhouse/agent-template-sources/plugin-{pluginId}-*/` (injected
templates)
- `.clubhouse/plugin-data/_agentconfig:{pluginId}/` (project-level
injection metadata)

### No management UI exists

- **PluginListSettings.tsx** — shows plugin list with
enable/disable/permissions, but does not display what the plugin has
injected
- **ProjectAgentDefaultsSection.tsx** — lets you manually edit project
instructions and permissions, but does not indicate which parts came
from plugins vs. were manually authored. The HTML comment markers (`<!--
plugin:buddy-system:start -->`) are invisible to most users
- **SourceSkillsSection.tsx** — shows skills but doesn't distinguish
plugin-injected skills from user-created ones
- There is no "Injections" or "Plugin Contributions" view anywhere in
settings

## Injection surface

Plugins can inject via the `AgentConfigAPI`
(`src/renderer/plugins/plugin-api-factory.ts:1453+`):

| Method | Persisted Location | Cleanup on Uninstall |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| `injectSkill()` | `.clubhouse/skills/plugin-{id}-{name}/` | **None** |
| `injectAgentTemplate()` |
`.clubhouse/agent-template-sources/plugin-{id}-{name}/` | **None** |
| `appendInstructions()` | `.clubhouse/settings.json` →
`agentDefaults.instructions` | **None** |
| `addPermissionAllowRules()` | `.clubhouse/settings.json` →
`agentDefaults.permissions.allow[]` | **None** |
| `addPermissionDenyRules()` | `.clubhouse/settings.json` →
`agentDefaults.permissions.deny[]` | **None** |
| `injectMcpServers()` | `.clubhouse/settings.json` →
`agentDefaults.mcpJson` | **None** |

## Expected Behavior

1. **On uninstall**, the handler should clean up all project-level
injections for the plugin being removed (instructions, skills,
templates, permissions, MCP servers)
2. **In Settings**, there should be a view showing what each plugin has
injected (skills, instructions, permission rules, MCP servers) with the
ability to remove individual injections or all injections from a given
plugin
3. Orphaned injections from plugins that are no longer installed should
be flagged or auto-cleaned

## Workaround

Manual cleanup required:
